title: Definite Articles: le, la, les
description: Definite articles in French (le, la, les) are equivalent to "the" in English. They indicate a specific noun. "Le" is used for masculine singular nouns, "la" for feminine singular nouns, and "les" for plural nouns (both masculine and feminine).
examples: Le livre est sur la table. (The book is on the table.)
La pomme est rouge. (The apple is red.)
Les enfants jouent dans le parc. (The children are playing in the park.)

title: Indefinite Articles: un, une, des
description: Indefinite articles in French (un, une, des) are equivalent to "a/an" or "some" in English. "Un" is used for masculine singular nouns, "une" for feminine singular nouns, and "des" for plural nouns.
examples: J'ai un stylo. (I have a pen.)
Elle a une voiture. (She has a car.)
Nous avons des amis en France. (We have some friends in France.)

title: Present Tense of Regular -ER Verbs
description: Regular -ER verbs in French follow a pattern in the present tense. Remove the -ER ending and add the appropriate ending: -e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent.
examples: Je parle français. (I speak French.)
Tu parles anglais. (You speak English.)
Il/Elle parle espagnol. (He/She speaks Spanish.)
Nous parlons italien. (We speak Italian.)
Vous parlez allemand. (You speak German.)
Ils/Elles parlent portugais. (They speak Portuguese.)

title: Present Tense of Regular -IR Verbs
description: Regular -IR verbs in French follow a pattern in the present tense. Remove the -IR ending and add the appropriate ending: -is, -is, -it, -issons, -issez, -issent.
examples: Je finis mon travail. (I finish my work.)
Tu finis ton repas. (You finish your meal.)
Il/Elle finit son livre. (He/She finishes his/her book.)
Nous finissons nos devoirs. (We finish our homework.)
Vous finissez votre projet. (You finish your project.)
Ils/Elles finissent leurs exercices. (They finish their exercises.)

title: Present Tense of Regular -RE Verbs
description: Regular -RE verbs in French follow a pattern in the present tense. Remove the -RE ending and add the appropriate ending: -s, -s, -, -ons, -ez, -ent.
examples: Je vends ma voiture. (I sell my car.)
Tu vends ton vélo. (You sell your bike.)
Il/Elle vend sa maison. (He/She sells his/her house.)
Nous vendons nos livres. (We sell our books.)
Vous vendez vos meubles. (You sell your furniture.)
Ils/Elles vendent leurs jouets. (They sell their toys.)

title: Basic Negation: ne ... pas
description: To make a sentence negative in French, place "ne" before the verb and "pas" after it. In informal spoken French, "ne" is often omitted.
examples: Je ne parle pas chinois. (I don't speak Chinese.)
Elle n'aime pas le café. (She doesn't like coffee.)
Nous ne sommes pas fatigués. (We are not tired.)

title: Possessive Adjectives
description: Possessive adjectives in French (mon, ton, son, etc.) agree in gender and number with the noun they modify, not with the possessor as in English.
examples: Mon livre (my book - masculine)
Ma maison (my house - feminine)
Mes parents (my parents - plural)
Ton ami (your friend - masculine)
Ta voiture (your car - feminine)
Tes chaussures (your shoes - plural)

title: Forming Questions with Est-ce que
description: One way to form questions in French is to add 'est-ce que' at the beginning of a statement. The word order remains the same as in the statement.
examples: Tu parles français. → Est-ce que tu parles français? (Do you speak French?)
Il est médecin. → Est-ce qu'il est médecin? (Is he a doctor?)
Vous aimez le chocolat. → Est-ce que vous aimez le chocolat? (Do you like chocolate?)

title: Forming Questions with Inversion
description: Another way to form questions in French is to invert the subject and verb. If the verb ends with a vowel, add -t- between the verb and subject pronoun for euphony.
examples: Tu parles français. → Parles-tu français? (Do you speak French?)
Il est médecin. → Est-il médecin? (Is he a doctor?)
Elle va au cinéma. → Va-t-elle au cinéma? (Is she going to the cinema?)

title: Adjective Agreement
description: In French, adjectives must agree in gender and number with the nouns they modify. Most adjectives add -e for feminine and -s for plural.
examples: Un petit garçon (a small boy) → Une petite fille (a small girl)
Un grand livre (a big book) → Une grande maison (a big house)
Un ami intelligent (an intelligent friend) → Des amis intelligents (intelligent friends)

title: Prepositions with Countries and Cities
description: Different prepositions are used with countries and cities in French. Use 'à' for cities, 'en' for feminine countries, 'au' for masculine countries, and 'aux' for plural countries.
examples: Je vais à Paris. (I'm going to Paris.)
Il habite en France. (He lives in France. - feminine)
Nous voyageons au Canada. (We are traveling to Canada. - masculine)
Ils sont aux États-Unis. (They are in the United States. - plural)

title: Passé Composé with Avoir
description: The passé composé is a past tense used to express completed actions. It's formed with the present tense of "avoir" + past participle. For regular -ER verbs, the past participle is formed by replacing -er with -é.
examples: J'ai parlé avec Marie hier. (I spoke with Marie yesterday.)
Tu as mangé au restaurant. (You ate at the restaurant.)
Nous avons regardé un film. (We watched a movie.)

title: Passé Composé with Être
description: Some verbs use "être" instead of "avoir" in the passé composé. These are mainly verbs of movement or change of state. The past participle must agree in gender and number with the subject.
examples: Je suis allé(e) au cinéma. (I went to the cinema.)
Elle est arrivée en retard. (She arrived late.)
Ils sont partis tôt. (They left early.)

title: Futur Proche (Near Future)
description: The futur proche expresses actions that will happen in the near future. It's formed with the present tense of "aller" + infinitive.
examples: Je vais étudier ce soir. (I'm going to study tonight.)
Nous allons visiter Paris. (We're going to visit Paris.)
Ils vont préparer le dîner. (They're going to prepare dinner.)

title: Direct Object Pronouns
description: Direct object pronouns (me, te, le, la, nous, vous, les) replace direct objects to avoid repetition. They are placed before the verb in simple tenses and before the auxiliary in compound tenses.
examples: Je vois Marie. → Je la vois. (I see Marie. → I see her.)
Tu manges une pomme. → Tu la manges. (You eat an apple. → You eat it.)
Nous aimons nos amis. → Nous les aimons. (We love our friends. → We love them.)

title: Indirect Object Pronouns
description: Indirect object pronouns (me, te, lui, nous, vous, leur) replace indirect objects to avoid repetition. They are placed before the verb in simple tenses and before the auxiliary in compound tenses.
examples: Je parle à Marie. → Je lui parle. (I speak to Marie. → I speak to her.)
Tu téléphones à tes parents. → Tu leur téléphones. (You call your parents. → You call them.)
Il donne un cadeau à son ami. → Il lui donne un cadeau. (He gives a gift to his friend. → He gives him a gift.)

title: Comparative Adjectives
description: To compare things in French, use 'plus...que' (more...than), 'moins...que' (less...than), or 'aussi...que' (as...as) with adjectives.
examples: Pierre est plus grand que Paul. (Pierre is taller than Paul.)
Cette voiture est moins chère que celle-là. (This car is less expensive than that one.)
Mon appartement est aussi spacieux que le tien. (My apartment is as spacious as yours.)

title: Superlative Adjectives
description: To express the superlative in French, use 'le/la/les plus' or 'le/la/les moins' before the adjective. The article agrees with the noun being described.
examples: C'est le plus grand bâtiment de la ville. (It's the tallest building in the city.)
Elle est la plus intelligente de la classe. (She is the most intelligent in the class.)
Ce sont les moins chers des hôtels. (These are the least expensive of the hotels.)

title: Demonstrative Adjectives
description: Demonstrative adjectives (ce, cet, cette, ces) point out specific nouns. 'Ce' is used before masculine singular nouns beginning with a consonant, 'cet' before masculine singular nouns beginning with a vowel, 'cette' before feminine singular nouns, and 'ces' before plural nouns.
examples: Ce livre est intéressant. (This book is interesting.)
Cet homme est mon père. (This man is my father.)
Cette femme est ma mère. (This woman is my mother.)
Ces enfants sont mes cousins. (These children are my cousins.)

title: Imparfait (Imperfect Tense)
description: The imparfait is used to describe ongoing or habitual actions in the past, or to set the scene. It's formed by taking the nous form of the present tense, dropping -ons, and adding the endings: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient.
examples: Quand j'étais petit, j'habitais à la campagne. (When I was young, I used to live in the countryside.)
Il pleuvait quand je suis sorti. (It was raining when I went out.)
Nous mangions souvent au restaurant. (We often used to eat at the restaurant.)

title: Conditional Present
description: The conditional present expresses hypothetical actions or polite requests. It's formed using the same stem as the future tense + the endings: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient.
examples: Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît. (I would like a coffee, please.)
Nous pourrions aller au cinéma. (We could go to the cinema.)
S'il faisait beau, j'irais à la plage. (If the weather was nice, I would go to the beach.)

title: Relative Pronouns: qui, que, où, dont
description: Relative pronouns connect clauses and replace nouns. "Qui" replaces the subject, "que" replaces the direct object, "où" indicates place or time, and "dont" expresses possession or replaces "de" + noun.
examples: La femme qui parle est ma professeure. (The woman who is speaking is my teacher.)
Le livre que je lis est intéressant. (The book that I'm reading is interesting.)
La ville où j'habite est petite. (The city where I live is small.)
L'homme dont je parle est célèbre. (The man about whom I'm speaking is famous.)

title: Future Simple
description: The future simple expresses actions that will happen in the future. For regular verbs, it's formed by adding the endings -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont to the infinitive. For -re verbs, drop the final -e before adding the endings.
examples: Je parlerai français couramment un jour. (I will speak French fluently one day.)
Tu finiras ton travail demain. (You will finish your work tomorrow.)
Nous vendrons notre maison l'année prochaine. (We will sell our house next year.)

title: Pronouns Y and EN
description: The pronoun 'y' replaces a place or a thing introduced by 'à' or another preposition of place. The pronoun 'en' replaces a quantity or a thing introduced by 'de'.
examples: Je vais à Paris. → J'y vais. (I'm going to Paris. → I'm going there.)
Elle parle de son travail. → Elle en parle. (She talks about her work. → She talks about it.)
J'ai besoin de trois livres. → J'en ai besoin de trois. (I need three books. → I need three of them.)

title: Subjunctive Mood
description: The subjunctive is used to express doubt, desire, necessity, or emotions. It's often used after certain conjunctions and expressions like "il faut que" or "je veux que".
examples: Il faut que tu viennes demain. (You need to come tomorrow.)
Je suis content que vous soyez là. (I'm happy that you are here.)
Bien qu'il pleuve, nous sortons. (Although it's raining, we're going out.)

title: Plus-que-parfait (Pluperfect)
description: The plus-que-parfait expresses actions that happened before another past action. It's formed with the imparfait of the auxiliary verb (avoir or être) + past participle.
examples: J'avais déjà mangé quand il est arrivé. (I had already eaten when he arrived.)
Elle était partie avant que je ne téléphone. (She had left before I called.)
Nous avions étudié le français pendant trois ans avant de visiter Paris. (We had studied French for three years before visiting Paris.)

title: Passive Voice
description: The passive voice shifts focus from the doer to the receiver of an action. It's formed with être + past participle, which must agree with the subject.
examples: Le livre a été écrit par Victor Hugo. (The book was written by Victor Hugo.)
La lettre sera envoyée demain. (The letter will be sent tomorrow.)
Les règles sont expliquées par le professeur. (The rules are explained by the teacher.)

title: Reported Speech
description: Reported speech is used to report what someone else said without quoting them directly. In French, tenses, pronouns, and time expressions change in reported speech.
examples: Elle dit: "Je suis fatiguée." → Elle dit qu'elle est fatiguée. (She says: "I am tired." → She says that she is tired.)
Il a dit: "J'ai fini mon travail." → Il a dit qu'il avait fini son travail. (He said: "I have finished my work." → He said that he had finished his work.)
Ils ont demandé: "Viendrez-vous demain?" → Ils ont demandé si nous viendrions le lendemain. (They asked: "Will you come tomorrow?" → They asked if we would come the next day.)

title: Conditional Past
description: The conditional past expresses hypothetical actions in the past or regrets. It's formed with the conditional present of the auxiliary verb (avoir or être) + past participle.
examples: J'aurais aimé visiter le musée. (I would have liked to visit the museum.)
Si j'avais su, je serais venu plus tôt. (If I had known, I would have come earlier.)
Ils auraient pu nous prévenir. (They could have warned us.)

title: Futur Antérieur (Future Perfect)
description: The futur antérieur expresses actions that will be completed before another future action. It's formed with the future simple of the auxiliary verb (avoir or être) + past participle.
examples: J'aurai fini mon travail avant midi. (I will have finished my work before noon.)
Quand tu arriveras, elle sera déjà partie. (When you arrive, she will have already left.)
Nous aurons préparé le dîner avant votre arrivée. (We will have prepared dinner before your arrival.)

title: Gérondif (Gerund)
description: The gérondif expresses simultaneity, manner, or cause. It's formed with "en" + present participle (stem of nous form in present tense + -ant).
examples: Elle chante en prenant sa douche. (She sings while taking her shower.)
Il a appris le français en regardant des films. (He learned French by watching movies.)
En travaillant dur, vous réussirez. (By working hard, you will succeed.)

title: Subjonctif Passé (Past Subjunctive)
description: The subjonctif passé expresses completed actions in contexts requiring the subjunctive. It's formed with the present subjunctive of the auxiliary verb (avoir or être) + past participle.
examples: Je doute qu'il ait compris la question. (I doubt that he understood the question.)
Bien qu'elle soit arrivée en retard, elle a pu assister à la réunion. (Although she arrived late, she was able to attend the meeting.)
Je suis content que vous ayez réussi votre examen. (I'm happy that you passed your exam.)

title: Propositions Participes (Participle Clauses)
description: Participle clauses can replace subordinate clauses to make sentences more concise. They can be formed with present or past participles.
examples: Ayant fini son travail, il est rentré chez lui. (Having finished his work, he went home.)
Les étudiants, sachant la réponse, ont levé la main. (The students, knowing the answer, raised their hands.)
Une fois arrivée à la gare, elle a appelé un taxi. (Once arrived at the station, she called a taxi.)

title: Discours Indirect au Passé (Reported Speech in the Past)
description: When reporting speech in the past, tenses shift backward in time. Present becomes imparfait, passé composé becomes plus-que-parfait, future becomes conditional, etc.
examples: Il a dit: "Je suis malade." → Il a dit qu'il était malade. (He said: "I am sick." → He said that he was sick.)
Elle a dit: "J'ai fini mon travail." → Elle a dit qu'elle avait fini son travail. (She said: "I have finished my work." → She said that she had finished her work.)
Ils ont dit: "Nous viendrons demain." → Ils ont dit qu'ils viendraient le lendemain. (They said: "We will come tomorrow." → They said that they would come the next day.)

title: Literary Tenses: Passé Simple
description: The passé simple is a literary past tense used in formal writing instead of the passé composé. It's formed by adding specific endings to the verb stem, which vary by verb group.
examples: Il parla pendant deux heures. (He spoke for two hours.)
Nous finîmes notre travail à minuit. (We finished our work at midnight.)
Les enfants coururent vers le parc. (The children ran toward the park.)

title: Literary Tenses: Imparfait du Subjonctif
description: The imparfait du subjonctif is a literary subjunctive tense used in formal writing. It's formed from the passé simple of the third person plural, dropping -rent and adding specific endings.
examples: Il fallait qu'il parlât plus fort. (It was necessary that he speak louder.)
Bien qu'elle fût malade, elle vint à la réunion. (Although she was sick, she came to the meeting.)
Je doutais qu'ils pussent terminer à temps. (I doubted that they could finish on time.)
